agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement. The compliance mandate for immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, and field-level change tracking maps directly to event sourcing's core properties. However, event sourcing is not the only viable path — a simpler Event Log + CQRS hybrid (append-only event table alongside the current mutable state table) delivers 90% of the compliance value at 40% of the complexity. The recommendation is the hybrid approach for teams without prior event sourcing experience, or full event sourcing if the team has the maturity and the domain complexity warrants it.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Command Handler","responsibility":"Validates and accepts loan application mutations (status changes, data edits). Enforces business rules before emitting events.","boundaries":"Owns write-side logic. Does not query read models. Rejects invalid state transitions."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all domain events. Each event captures who, what, when, and the before/after delta.","boundaries":"Write-once, read-many. No UPDATE or DELETE operations permitted. Append only."},{"name":"Loan Application Aggregate","responsibility":"Reconstructs current state by replaying events. Enforces invariants (e.g., cannot approve a withdrawn application). Emits new events.","boundaries":"Consistency boundary for a single loan application. One aggregate per application ID."},{"name":"Read Model Projector","responsibility":"Subscribes to events and projects them into query-optimized read models (current state table, audit trail view, point-in-time reconstruction view).","boundaries":"Eventually consistent with the event store. Can be rebuilt from scratch by replaying events."},{"name":"Audit Query Service","responsibility":"Serves compliance queries: full history of an application, state-at-time-T reconstruction, field-level change reports.","boundaries":"Read-only. Queries read models, never the event store directly for operational queries."},{"name":"Snapshot Store","responsibility":"Periodically persists materialized aggregate state to avoid replaying full event history for long-lived applications.","boundaries":"Performance optimization only. Never the source of truth — events remain authoritative."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Command Handler","to":"Loan Application Aggregate","contract":"LoadAggregate(applicationId) → Aggregate; Aggregate.apply(Command) → Event[]","coupling_type":"direct synchronous"},{"from":"Loan Application Aggregate","to":"Event Store","contract":"append(applicationId, expectedVersion, Event[]) → void. Optimistic concurrency via version check.","coupling_type":"direct synchronous"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Read Model Projector","contract":"Event stream subscription (ordered, at-least-once delivery). Events are immutable facts.","coupling_type":"async event-driven"},{"from":"Read Model Projector","to":"Audit Query Service","contract":"Projected read models in SQL tables optimized for compliance queries.","coupling_type":"indirect via shared database"},{"from":"Loan Application Aggregate","to":"Snapshot Store","contract":"saveSnapshot(applicationId, version, state); loadSnapshot(applicationId) → (version, state)?","coupling_type":"direct synchronous"}],"data_flow":"1. User submits change (e.g., update income field, change status to 'approved'). 2. Command Handler loads the Loan Application Aggregate from Event Store (replay events from last snapshot). 3. Aggregate validates the command against current state and business rules. 4. If valid, Aggregate emits event(s) (e.g., LoanFieldChanged{field:'income', old:'50000', new:'75000', changedBy:'agent-42', timestamp:...}). 5. Event Store appends event(s) with optimistic concurrency check. 6. Read Model Projector asynchronously consumes new events and updates: (a) current-state table for operational queries, (b) audit-trail table for compliance, (c) temporal state table for point-in-time reconstruction. 7. Audit Query Service reads from projected models to serve compliance requests.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing","rationale":"The compliance requirement IS event sourcing's value proposition: immutable history, full audit trail, point-in-time reconstruction. The domain events become the regulatory record."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Separating write (event append) from read (projected views) allows optimizing audit queries without compromising the write model's integrity. Compliance queries have very different access patterns than operational writes."},{"pattern":"Aggregate Pattern","rationale":"Each loan application is a natural consistency boundary. Business rules (valid status transitions, required fields before approval) are enforced per-application."},{"pattern":"Snapshotting","rationale":"Loan applications can accumulate hundreds of events over months of processing. Snapshots prevent replay performance degradation."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency","rationale":"Version-based concurrency on the event stream prevents lost updates without pessimistic locking, which would harm throughput."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["LoanApplication — root aggregate. Contains application data fields, status, assigned agents. Emits: ApplicationCreated, ApplicationFieldChanged, ApplicationStatusChanged, ApplicationAssigned, ApplicationSubmitted, ApplicationApproved, ApplicationDenied, ApplicationWithdrawn."]},{"name":"Audit & Compliance","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["AuditTrail — read model projected from Loan Application events. No write operations of its own. Serves compliance queries and regulatory reports."]},{"name":"Identity & Access Management","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User/Agent — provides the 'who' (actor identity) attached to every event. Use existing IAM system; do not rebuild."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Loan Application Management","downstream":"Audit & Compliance","relationship":"Published Language — Loan Application Management publishes domain events in a well-defined schema. Audit & Compliance subscribes and projects. No upstream dependency on downstream."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access Management","downstream":"Loan Application Management","relationship":"Conformist — Loan Application Management conforms to IAM's user/agent identity model. Actor IDs from IAM are embedded in every event."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Full Event Sourcing vs. Append-Only Audit Log alongside mutable state","gains":"Event sourcing gives authoritative, reconstructable state from events alone. No drift between audit log and actual state — they are the same thing. Eliminates an entire class of bugs where the audit log disagrees with current state.","sacrifices":"Higher complexity: team must learn event sourcing patterns, handle eventual consistency, manage snapshots, deal with event schema evolution. Operational burden increases.","alternatives_considered":["Append-only audit_log table with triggers on the existing loan_applications table. Simpler but creates two sources of truth that can diverge. Triggers can be bypassed. Cannot reconstruct state without complex logic.","CDC (Change Data Capture) via Debezium or similar. Captures all changes at the database level. Good for audit but loses business intent — you see column changes, not 'application was approved'. Harder for compliance teams to interpret.","Temporal database (e.g., PostgreSQL temporal tables, SQL Server temporal). Built-in history but limited to row-level snapshots. No business event semantics. Vendor lock-in concerns."]},{"decision":"Asynchronous read model projection vs. synchronous","gains":"Write path stays fast. Read models can be optimized independently. Can add new projections without touching the write path.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency means a just-written event may not appear in the audit view for milliseconds to seconds. For compliance queries (which are inherently retrospective), this is acceptable.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous projection: simpler mental model but couples write performance to read model complexity. As audit queries grow, write latency increases."]},{"decision":"Event schema includes before/after field values vs. just new values","gains":"Field-level diff is immediately available without replaying prior events. Compliance team can see exactly what changed in each event.","sacrifices":"Larger event payload. Slight increase in command handler complexity to capture old values before applying changes.","alternatives_considered":["Store only new values and compute diffs by comparing consecutive events. Saves storage but makes compliance queries expensive and complex."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Read model projections","evolution_path":"New compliance requirements (e.g., new report formats, new regulatory queries) can be met by adding new projections that replay existing events. Zero changes to the write side."},{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Use explicit event versioning (v1, v2) with upcasters that transform old events to new schemas during replay. Supports schema changes without migrating historical data."},{"area":"Integration with external systems","evolution_path":"Events can be published to message brokers (Kafka, SNS) for downstream consumers: fraud detection, analytics, regulatory reporting systems. The event store becomes an integration backbone."},{"area":"Retention and archival","evolution_path":"Old events can be archived to cold storage while snapshots maintain operational performance. Full history remains available for regulatory audits via archive retrieval."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event immutability","reason":"Events must NEVER be modified or deleted once written. This is both the core value proposition (compliance) and the core constraint. Any 'correction' must be a new compensating event, not a mutation. The team must internalize this — there is no UPDATE on the event store."},{"area":"Event schema — core fields","reason":"Every event must include: aggregate ID, sequence number, timestamp, actor ID, event type, and payload. This schema is load-bearing for compliance and must not be relaxed."},{"area":"Aggregate consistency boundary","reason":"One loan application = one aggregate. Cross-application consistency (e.g., 'total loan exposure per borrower') must be handled via read models or sagas, not by expanding the aggregate boundary."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Event schema evolution complexity. As business requirements change, event schemas will evolve. Without a disciplined versioning and upcasting strategy, the system becomes brittle.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Establish event versioning conventions from day one. Use an event registry with JSON Schema validation. Implement upcasters that transform old event versions during replay. Never break backward compatibility — only add fields, never remove."},{"risk":"Team inexperience with event sourcing. Event sourcing has a steep learning curve. Misapplied, it creates more problems than it solves (e.g., treating events as CRUD logs, putting too much in one aggregate, ignoring eventual consistency).","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in team training before implementation. Start with the loan application domain only — do not adopt event sourcing system-wide. Consider the hybrid approach (append-only event log + mutable state) if the team is not ready."},{"risk":"Read model projection lag causing confusion. Operators may see stale data in audit views immediately after a change.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Display 'last projected event timestamp' in audit UIs. For critical compliance queries, offer a 'consistent read' option that queries the event store directly and rebuilds state on the fly."},{"risk":"Event store performance at scale. A high-volume lending operation could generate millions of events. Naive implementations degrade.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a purpose-built event store (EventStoreDB, Marten, or a well-indexed PostgreSQL append-only table with partitioning). Implement snapshots for aggregates with >100 events. Partition by application ID."},{"risk":"Regulatory requirement to 'delete' data (e.g., GDPR right to erasure) conflicts with event immutability.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in events with per-borrower keys. To 'delete', destroy the key. Events remain but PII becomes unrecoverable. Design this from day one — retrofitting is extremely painful."},{"risk":"Compensating events for corrections may confuse compliance auditors who expect a simple linear history.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Build audit views that present a 'clean' timeline showing corrections inline (e.g., 'Income corrected from X to Y on date Z, original entry was on date W'). The raw event stream stays immutable; the projection presents it clearly."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement an append-only events table in the existing PostgreSQL database alongside the current loan_applications table. Each mutation writes an event AND updates current state in a single transaction. This gives immediate audit trail compliance with minimal architectural disruption. Phase 2: Once the team is comfortable with event-driven thinking, migrate to true event sourcing where events become the sole source of truth and current state is derived. Phase 3: Add CQRS read models for specialized compliance queries. This phased approach de-risks the transition while delivering compliance value immediately.","files_to_create_or_modify":["loan_application_events (new table): id UUID PK, application_id UUID FK, sequence_number INT, event_type VARCHAR, event_version INT, payload JSONB, actor_id UUID, actor_type VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, metadata JSONB. Constraints: UNIQUE(application_id, sequence_number), no UPDATE/DELETE grants.","LoanApplicationEvent (domain event base class): defines common fields — applicationId, sequenceNumber, occurredAt, actorId, eventType.","Concrete event classes: ApplicationCreated, ApplicationFieldChanged (field, oldValue, newValue), ApplicationStatusChanged (oldStatus, newStatus, reason), ApplicationAssigned, etc.","LoanApplicationCommandHandler: validates commands, loads aggregate, applies command, persists events.","LoanApplicationAggregate: rebuilds state from events, enforces invariants, emits new events.","AuditTrailProjection: event handler that maintains a denormalized audit view table.","PointInTimeQueryService: given an applicationId and timestamp, replays events up to that point to reconstruct state."],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Decider pattern for command handling: (state, command) → events and (state, event) → state. This keeps the aggregate logic pure and testable. All events are past-tense named facts (ApplicationApproved, not ApproveApplication). Every event carries actor identity and timestamp. Use JSONB for event payloads to allow schema flexibility with JSON Schema validation for safety.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use database triggers for event generation — they bypass domain logic and lose business intent.","Do NOT store derived/computed fields in events — store facts, compute derived values in projections.","Do NOT create one giant 'ApplicationUpdated' event — use fine-grained events that capture business intent (FieldChanged, StatusChanged, etc.).","Do NOT skip optimistic concurrency — without it, concurrent updates can produce an inconsistent event stream.","Do NOT treat the event store as a message queue — it is a persistence mechanism. Use a separate pub/sub system for cross-service communication.","Do NOT forget crypto-shredding for PII — retrofitting this is a nightmare in regulated environments.","Do NOT expand the aggregate boundary to include multiple applications — cross-application concerns belong in read models or process managers."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong fit for this specific compliance requirement — the alignment between the pattern's properties and the regulatory needs is direct.","The phased migration approach (audit log → event sourcing → CQRS) de-risks adoption.","Crypto-shredding for GDPR/data deletion is a well-established pattern in event-sourced financial systems.","The aggregate boundary (one per loan application) is clearly correct."],"low_confidence_areas":["Team readiness for event sourcing — without knowing the team's experience level, the recommendation between full event sourcing and the hybrid approach is hedged.","Exact event granularity — the right level of event granularity depends on specific compliance requirements that would need detailed analysis with the compliance team.","Infrastructure choices (EventStoreDB vs PostgreSQL-based) depend on scale, ops maturity, and existing infrastructure that are not specified."],"assumptions":["The team is using a relational database (PostgreSQL assumed) and can add tables.","Loan applications have a bounded lifecycle (months, not decades) so event counts per aggregate remain manageable with snapshots.","The compliance requirement is primarily retrospective (audit after the fact) not real-time monitoring.","GDPR or similar data deletion requirements apply given financial services context."]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement. The compliance mandate for immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, and field-level change tracking maps directly to event sourcing's core properties. However, event sourcing is not the only viable path — a simpler Event Log + CQRS hybrid (append-only event table alongside the current mutable state table) delivers 90% of the compliance value at 40% of the complexity. The
